title: Rest, sleep and positioning
summary: Sleep and reduced workload support recovery and make symptoms easier to tolerate. Positioning can improve comfort, but strict bed rest or forcing sleep is unnecessary.
evidenceSnapshot: Direct cold-treatment trials are limited, but sleep is strongly linked to immune regulation and symptom tolerance. Elevation is a comfort strategy rather than a cure.

interventions.name: Adequate sleep
interventions.finding: Supports immune function, mood and pain tolerance during illness.
interventions.practical: Protect a normal bedtime, reduce evening stimulation and nap briefly if needed.
interventions.mechanism: Sleep coordinates immune signalling and recovery processes.
interventions.cautions: Sedating cold medicines can impair driving and interact with alcohol or other sedatives.

interventions.name: Upper-body elevation
interventions.finding: May reduce post-nasal drip, reflux-triggered cough and the sensation of blockage while lying down.
interventions.practical: Raise the upper torso with a wedge or adjust the bed; avoid piling loose pillows around infants.
interventions.cautions: Infants should sleep flat on a firm, safe sleep surface; do not incline infant sleep products.

interventions.name: Side sleeping
interventions.finding: Some adults breathe more comfortably on one side or with the more open nostril uppermost.
interventions.practical: Use the position that feels easiest.
interventions.mechanism: Nasal cycling and gravity change congestion between nostrils.
interventions.cautions: No special position treats the virus.

interventions.name: Prolonged complete bed rest
interventions.finding: May worsen stiffness, sleep timing and deconditioning after the acute phase.
interventions.practical: Get up for fluids, toileting and brief gentle movement when safe.
interventions.cautions: Stop activity if dizzy, faint or breathless.

practicalSteps: Create a simple night setup: water, tissues, thermometer and medicines log.
Keep the room comfortably cool and dark.
Use a shower or saline before bed rather than repeatedly waking for multiple remedies.
Resume normal activity in stages as fever and systemic symptoms settle.
avoidOrCare: Do not give sedating antihistamines to children to make them sleep.
Persistent inability to sleep because of breathlessness, chest pain or severe cough needs assessment.

myths.claim: You must stay in bed until every symptom is gone.
myths.reality: Rest matters, but gentle movement is reasonable once fever and severe symptoms have eased.
Elevation may improve comfort but does not remove the virus.

redFlags: Repeated waking gasping or with chest pain
Infant placed in an unsafe inclined sleep position
unsettled: There is little direct evidence comparing specific sleep positions for ordinary viral upper-respiratory infections.
